book.title_ar: الفيزياء 2 - التعليم الثانوي - نظام المسارات - السنة الثانية
book.title_en: Physics 2 - Secondary Education - Pathways System - Year 2
book.page_note: PDF page number == printed page number. Cover is page 1.

chapters.summary: يربط هذا الفصل بين قوانين كبلر لحركة الكواكب وقانون نيوتن للجذب الكوني، ويستخدمه لوصف مدارات الكواكب والأقمار الاصطناعية وفهم طبيعة قوة الجاذبية.

lessons.summary: يقدم نموذج كوبرنيكوس الشمسي وقوانين كبلر الثلاثة، ويشتق منها قانون نيوتن للجذب الكوني، ويحسب الزمن الدوري والسرعة المدارية للكواكب والأقمار.
objectives: الربط بين قوانين كبلر وقانون الجذب الكوني
حساب الزمن الدوري ومقدار السرعة المدارية

lessons.title_en: Using the Law of Universal Gravitation
lessons.summary: يطبق قانون الجذب الكوني على مدارات الكواكب والأقمار الاصطناعية، ويفسر انعدام الوزن في السقوط الحر، ويميز بين كتلة القصور وكتلة الجاذبية، ويقارن بين نظرتي نيوتن وآينشتاين للجاذبية.
objectives: ربط انعدام الوزن بأجسام في حالة سقوط حر
المقارنة بين كتلة القصور وكتلة الجاذبية
المقارنة بين وجهتي نظر نيوتن وآينشتاين حول الجاذبية

concepts.concept_ar: كتلة القصور وكتلة الجاذبية ومبدأ التكافؤ

chapters.summary: يصف الحركة الدورانية وقياسها بالإزاحة والسرعة والتسارع الزاوي، ويفسر كيف يغير العزم السرعة الزاوية، ويحلل مركز الكتلة وشروط الاتزان والقوى الظاهرية في الأطر المرجعية الدوارة.

lessons.summary: يقدم الراديان كوحدة لقياس الزوايا، ويعرف الإزاحة الزاوية والسرعة الزاوية المتجهة والتسارع الزاوي، ويستخدمها لحل مسائل الحركة الدورانية.
يعرف العزم وذراع القوة، ويوضح كيف يعتمد العزم على مقدار القوة وذراعها، ويحسب محصلة العزوم المؤثرة في جسم.
objectives: تحديد العوامل التي يعتمد عليها العزم

lessons.summary: يعرف مركز الكتلة وكيفية تحديد موقعه، ويحلل شروط الاتزان وتأثير موقع مركز الكتلة في استقرار الأجسام وانقلابها، ويناقش القوى الظاهرية كالطاردة المركزية وقوة كوريوليس في الأطر المرجعية الدوارة.
objectives: توضيح تأثير موقع مركز الكتلة في استقرار الجسم
وصف كيف يؤدي دوران الأطر المرجعية إلى ظهور قوى ظاهرية

chapters.summary: يقدم مفهومي الزخم والدفع ونظرية الدفع-الزخم، ويربط القانون الثالث لنيوتن بقانون حفظ الزخم في الأنظمة المغلقة والمعزولة، ويطبقه على تصادمات الأجسام.

lessons.summary: يعرف الزخم بوصفه حاصل ضرب الكتلة في السرعة، ويعرف الدفع بوصفه حاصل ضرب القوة في زمن تأثيرها، ويربطهما عبر نظرية الدفع-الزخم لتفسير ما يحدث في التصادمات.
objectives: تحديد مقدار الدفع الواقع على جسم

lessons.summary: يستنتج قانون حفظ الزخم من القانون الثالث لنيوتن، ويميز بين الأنظمة المغلقة والمعزولة، ويستخدم القانون لحل مسائل التصادم بين جسمين أو أكثر.
objectives: الربط بين القانون الثالث لنيوتن وحفظ الزخم

chapters.summary: يميز بين الشغل والقدرة، ويربطهما بتغير طاقة النظام، ويفسر كيف تقلل الآلات البسيطة والمركبة القوة اللازمة لإنجاز الشغل ويحسب كفاءتها وفائدتها الميكانيكية.

lessons.summary: يعرف الشغل والطاقة الحركية ويستخدم نظرية الشغل-الطاقة لربطهما، ثم يعرف القدرة بوصفها معدل بذل الشغل ويحسب القدرة المستهلكة بالواط.
يوضح فوائد الآلات البسيطة في تغيير مقدار القوة أو اتجاهها، ويعرف الفائدة الميكانيكية (الفعلية والمثالية) والكفاءة، ويحلل الآلات المركبة المكونة من آلات بسيطة.
objectives: المقارنة بين كفاءة الآلات المثالية والحقيقية
تحليل الآلات المركبة مبيناً الآلات البسيطة التي تتكون منها

chapters.summary: يعرف الطاقة كخاصية للجسم تتغير بتغير موقعه أو سرعته، ويستعرض أشكالها المختلفة (حركية، وضع جاذبية، وضع مرونية)، ويطبق قانون حفظ الطاقة على الأنظمة المغلقة وعلى التصادمات بأنواعها.

lessons.summary: يستخدم نموذج الشغل-الطاقة لتعرف الأشكال المختلفة للطاقة: الحركية الانتقالية والدورانية، طاقة الوضع الجاذبية (مع تحديد مستوى الإسناد)، وطاقة الوضع المرونية المخزنة في الأجسام المرنة.
objectives: تحديد طاقة الوضع الجاذبية لنظام ما
تبيين كيفية تخزين طاقة الوضع المرونية

lessons.summary: يقرر قانون حفظ الطاقة في الأنظمة المعزولة، ويعرف الطاقة الميكانيكية (E = KE + PE)، ويستخدم القانون لحل مسائل الحركة وتحليل التصادمات المرنة والعديمة المرونة وفوق المرنة.
objectives: حل مسائل باستخدام قانون حفظ الطاقة
تحليل التصادمات لإيجاد التغير في الطاقة الحركية

chapters.summary: يربط الحرارة بطاقتي الوضع والحركة للذرات والجزيئات، ويميزها عن الشغل، ويحسب كمية الحرارة المنتقلة والممتصة، ويغطي تغيرات حالة المادة والقانونين الأول والثاني في الديناميكا الحرارية.

lessons.summary: يميز بين درجة الحرارة والطاقة الحرارية، ويصف انتقال الحرارة بالتوصيل والحمل والإشعاع، ويعرف الحرارة النوعية ويستخدمها لحساب كمية الحرارة المنتقلة والوصول إلى الاتزان الحراري.
objectives: وصف الطاقة الحرارية ومقارنتها بطاقة الوضع والطاقة الحركية
التمييز بين درجة الحرارة والطاقة الحرارية

lessons.title_ar: تغيرات حالة المادة وقوانين الديناميكا الحرارية
lessons.title_en: Changes of State and Laws of Thermodynamics
lessons.summary: يفسر تغيرات حالة المادة باستخدام الحرارة الكامنة للانصهار والتبخر، ويقدم القانون الأول للديناميكا الحرارية (حفظ الطاقة في الأنظمة الحرارية) والقانون الثاني ومفهوم الإنتروبي، ويميز بين الحرارة والشغل في المحركات الحرارية.
objectives: تعرف القانونين الأول والثاني في الديناميكا الحرارية

gravity2.concept_en: What is gravity? — foundational mental model
gravity2.handoff_ar: هذا الفيديو يبني الحدس: ما الجاذبية ولماذا تتجاذب الأجسام. نيوتن أعطانا معادلةً دقيقة لِـ«مقدار» قوة الجذب، أمّا «لماذا» فحكايةٌ أعمق (انحناء الزمكان). الآن لننتقل إلى ما يقوله الكتاب: قوانين كبلر، ثم قانون نيوتن للجذب الكوني.
beats: Forget planets for a second. Forget the sun, the moon, all of it. Picture two rocks floating in empty space.
Nothing's touching them. Nothing's pushing them. Just two rocks in the dark.
Watch.
The smaller one starts drifting. Toward the bigger one.
Nothing made it move. No wind, no magnet, no string. The big rock just pulled it. Quietly. Across empty space.
That's gravity. The whole thing. Anything with mass pulls anything else with mass. That's the rule.
It's not a planet thing. It's not even a space thing. Two coins on a table are pulling each other right now. You just can't feel it, because the pull is unimaginably tiny.
But in deep space, with nothing else around, that tiny pull is all there is. The rocks have nowhere to go but toward each other.
So they clump.
And the bigger they get, the more they pull. More rocks come in.
Give it a few million years.
And the lump becomes a ball.
That's a planet. Nobody built it. It built itself. Out of nothing but gravity and time.
Now zoom out further. Other rocks were doing the same thing nearby.
Same rule. Bigger pieces. The sun is just a rock that got so heavy it caught fire. The planets are rocks that didn't quite make it.
So when you look up and see planets in clean orbits — nobody arranged them. They fell into each other, billions of years ago, and what's left is what stuck.
Okay. But that just tells you what gravity does. It doesn't tell you why.
Why does a rock care about another rock? Why does mass pull anything at all?
Newton wrote a perfect equation for how strong the pull is. But the equation doesn't say why.
To answer that, we have to leave physics for a minute. Imagine a trampoline.
Put a bowling ball in the middle. The fabric sags around it.
Now roll a marble past it. The marble curves in.
Nothing is pulling the marble. It's just rolling on a surface that's been bent.
That's what gravity actually is. Heavy things bend the space around them. And everything else just rolls along the bend.
The two rocks weren't pulling each other. They were rolling into each other. Down a bend in space we couldn't see.
Nobody built the planets. They rolled into each other. Down the curve.

kepler3.handoff_ar: بعد أن بنى الفيديو التمهيدي الحدس، يشرح هذا الفيديو قانون كبلر الثالث ومعنى كلِّ رمز فيه (نصف القطر r، الزمن الدوري T، النسبة، التكعيب والتربيع). ثم انتقل إلى نص الكتاب لتطبيقه على أسئلة الدرس.
beats: Last time, you saw the planets slide into their orbits — down the bend in space. But look closer. Each planet keeps its own orbit, its own year. Mercury, close in, races. Neptune, far out, crawls.
Is that just chaos — every planet doing its own thing? Or is a planet's distance quietly setting how long its year is? There's only one way to find out. Measure.
It's not chaos. There's a rule — one clean rule, tying every planet's distance to its year. Find it, and one planet can tell you about another. Here's how.
Start with what you can actually measure. How far the planet sits from the sun. Call it r — the size of the orbit itself.
Then the time it takes to go all the way around, once. Call it T — the planet's year.
Now take two planets, not one. A and B. Not cryptic symbols — two worlds, held side by side. Earth and Mars.
That's what Kepler did, poring over Brahe's measurements. He wrote down r and T for planet after planet — and went hunting for the rule.
Try the ratio of the distances. r-A over r-B. How many times farther A sits than B.
And the ratio of the years. T-A over T-B. How many times longer A's year runs.
Cube the distances. Square the times. And the two sides come out exactly equal. That's the rule — Kepler's third law.
Why cubed, why squared? Double a planet's distance, and its year doesn't just double — it stretches further. Those exponents are exactly how much further.

So what's it for? Know how far a planet is, and you can predict its year — without ever timing it. Or measure its year, and pin down its distance. One world tells you another — not its mass, not the shape of its path, just how distance and time trade off.
Remember the bend. The farther out a planet sits, the gentler the slope, the slower it rolls. This equation is that bend — written in numbers.
No more weird codes. r is the orbit's width. T is the planet's year. A and B, two worlds. The exponents, the rhythm of the curve. Read it now — you can read it.

newton.handoff_ar: بعد أن بنى الفيديو التمهيدي حدسَ «لماذا»، يشرح هذا الفيديو قانون نيوتن في الجذب الكوني ومعنى كلِّ رمز — القوّة، الكتلتان، المسافة ومربّعها، وثابت الجاذبية. ثم انتقل إلى الكتاب لتطبيقه.
beats: In the teaser, you felt why two masses pull together — space bends, and they roll in. But how HARD do they pull? A pebble and a planet — surely not the same.
So what sets the strength of the pull? If we knew, we could predict the force between any two things in the universe.
It's not a mystery. The pull depends on just two things: how much mass, and how far apart. That's the whole story.
First, the masses. Call them m-one and m-two. More mass, more pull. Double either one, and the force doubles.
Then the distance between them. Call it r. Move them apart, and the pull weakens. But not gently — it drops off fast.
Why so fast? The pull spreads out over a sphere — and a sphere grows with the square of its radius. Double the distance, and the pull drops to a quarter.
One more piece. A number that sets gravity's overall strength — the same everywhere in the universe. Call it big G. It's tiny, which is why you don't feel two coins pull.
Put it together. Force equals big G, times the two masses multiplied, divided by the distance squared. Newton's law of universal gravitation.

What's it for? Hand it any two masses and a distance, and it gives you the exact pull between them — an apple and the Earth, the Earth and the Moon, two galaxies. Not the shape of the path, not why mass bends space — just how strong.
Remember the bend. A steeper bend means a stronger pull — and this equation is exactly how steep. The curve, written as a force.

No more weird codes. m-one and m-two, the two masses. r, how far apart. G, gravity's universal strength. The square, the spreading over a sphere. Read it — that's the pull between anything and anything.

orbital.handoff_ar: يبني هذا الفيديو على قانون نيوتن: مساواة الجذب بالقوّة المركزية يشتقّ زمن الدورة، ويعيد اكتشاف قانون كبلر الثالث. ثم طبّق من الكتاب.
beats: Newton gave you the force. Kepler gave you a pattern in the planets. Watch them meet.
A planet circles the sun, year after year — never falling in, never flying off. What sets the length of its year?
Gravity is the leash. The inward pull is exactly the force needed to bend a straight path into a circle. Set those two equal, and the year falls out.
On one side, Newton's pull: big G, the sun's mass, the planet's mass, over r squared. On the other, the turn a circle demands — which grows with speed and shrinks with radius.
The planet's own mass cancels — its year doesn't care how heavy it is. Solve for the time of one loop, and you get this.
Here, T is the planet's year. r, the size of its orbit. m-sun, the mass it circles. And G again, gravity's constant.
Push the planet farther out, and its year grows — fast. The distance is cubed under the root, so the year stretches by its cube root. Distance rules the calendar.

T equals two-pi times the square root of r cubed, over G times the sun's mass. Hand it an orbit's size and the sun's mass, and it predicts the year — for any planet, any satellite, any exoplanet you'll never visit.
And look — this IS Kepler's third law. What he found by staring at data, Newton's gravity now explains. Same bend, same rule — measured once, derived now.
No codes. T, the year. r, the orbit's size. m-sun, what it circles. G, gravity's constant. The cube and the root — distance ruling time. Read it: the law that times the heavens.

surfaceg.handoff_ar: يوجّه هذا الفيديو قانون نيوتن نحوك أنت والأرض ليفسّر تسارع الجاذبية g ولماذا لا يعتمد على كتلتك. ثم طبّق من الكتاب.
beats: Newton's law works for ANY two masses. Now point it at something personal — you, and the Earth under your feet.
You feel your weight right now, pressing into the floor. But what IS that, really?
Your weight is just Newton's pull — between your mass and the Earth's. Write 'your weight is m-g' on one side, and Newton's pull on the other. They're the same thing.
Your own mass sits on both sides — so it cancels. Gravity's pull per kilogram doesn't care how heavy you are. A feather and a hammer fall together.
What's left is g — the pull per kilogram at the surface. It depends only on the Earth's mass, and your distance from its center: its radius.
So g equals big G times the Earth's mass, over its radius squared. Put in the numbers, and out comes nine-point-eight — the number you've known all along.
What's it for? It tells you the surface gravity of ANY world. Swap in the Moon's mass and radius — you get one-sixth. Mars, Jupiter, a distant moon: same equation, new world.
It's the same bend. A heavier world digs a deeper well — and g is how steep that well is right at the surface.
No codes. g, the pull per kilogram. m-Earth, the world's mass. r-Earth, its radius. The square, again the sphere. Read it — that's why you weigh what you weigh.

weighearth.handoff_ar: يقلب هذا الفيديو معادلة تسارع الجاذبية ليزن الأرض من قيمٍ نعرفها (g ونصف القطر وثابت كافندش). ثم طبّق من الكتاب.
beats: You just found g equals big G times Earth's mass over its radius squared. Now do something audacious. Flip it around.
How could anyone weigh the entire Earth? You can't lift it. You can't put it on a scale. It seems impossible.
But you don't have to lift it. The equation already holds the Earth's mass inside it — just solve for that instead.
Multiply across, and the Earth's mass stands alone: it equals g, times the radius squared, divided by big G.

And every piece on the right, you already know. g is nine-point-eight — you measured it by dropping a stone. r-Earth — measured by the ancient Greeks. And big G — Cavendish, with two weights on a thread.
Put them in. Out comes six, times ten to the twenty-fourth, kilograms. You just weighed the planet — without ever lifting it.
And it doesn't stop at Earth. The same trick weighs the sun, the planets, anything you can orbit or stand near. Cavendish measured a thread in a room — and held a planet on a number.
No codes. m-Earth, the planet's mass — the thing we wanted. g and r, what we could measure standing on it. Big G, the thread in Cavendish's room. Read it: the equation that weighs worlds.

deprecated.note: Retired concept-pattern videos + projects. Kept for reference; not served by the tutor. Superseded by the prereq(gravity2)+equation(kepler3) model.

cavendish.concept_en: The Cavendish Experiment — weighing the Earth
cavendish.handoff_ar: The fiber, the masses, the deflection — that's the apparatus. Extracting the value of G from the measured twist, then using it to compute the Earth's mass — that's yours.
beats: Newton gave us the law. Every mass pulls every other mass.
But the law has a number in it. A constant. Big G. And nobody knew what it was.
For a hundred years, G was a question mark. The Earth's pull, we could feel. The constant behind it, we could not.
The problem: to measure G, you need to watch two ordinary objects pull each other. Not a planet. Just two weights.
And that pull is absurdly weak. Two lead spheres a foot apart attract each other with less force than the weight of a single grain of sand.
Cavendish's idea was patient. Hang two small masses from a thin fiber. Like a tiny barbell on a thread.
Bring two large masses close. The small ones drift toward them. Just barely.
The fiber twists. And how much it twists tells you exactly how hard they pulled.
From a twist you can barely see, Cavendish pulled out G.
And once you have G, the law works backward. The same equation that drops an apple can now weigh the entire planet.
He didn't visit space. He didn't lift the Earth. He measured a thread, in a room, and held a planet on a number.
The fiber, the masses, the deflection — that's the apparatus. Extracting the value of G from the measured twist, then using it to compute the Earth's mass — that's yours.

gfield.handoff_ar: The field is the map of the influence. Computing the field strength at a given distance from a mass, and the force it puts on an object placed there — that's yours.
beats: Drop something. It falls. We say the Earth pulls it.
But here's a strange question. How does the rock know the Earth is there? They never touch.
Newton himself was uneasy about this. Action at a distance, across empty space, with nothing in between.
So we change the picture. Instead of the Earth reaching out to grab things —
— the Earth changes the space around it. Everywhere. Whether anything is there to feel it or not.
That invisible influence has a name. The gravitational field. A property of the space, not of the rock.
The rock doesn't reach out to the Earth. It just responds to the field it's sitting in. Right here. Locally.
Closer to the Earth, the arrows crowd together. The field is stronger.
Far away, they thin out. Never quite zero. Just fainter, and fainter.
Weight isn't a thing an object owns. It's the field, times the mass, measured where the object happens to be.
Same rock. Different field. Different weight. Move it to the Moon and the arrows are weaker — the rock didn't change.
The field is the map of the influence. Computing the field strength at a given distance from a mass, and the force it puts on an object placed there — that's yours.

weightless.concept_en: Weightlessness & Free Fall (orbit as perpetual falling)
weightless.handoff_ar: Falling, with nothing to push back. How fast must you go to keep missing the ground? Set falling equal to curving — and solve.
beats: Astronauts float. The easy explanation: there's no gravity up there.
That explanation is wrong. At the space station's height, Earth's gravity is almost as strong as it is on the ground.
So if gravity is still pulling them — why don't they feel it?
Go back to Earth. An elevator. You stand on a scale. It reads your weight.
Now cut the cable. Elevator, scale, and you — all falling together.
The scale reads zero. Not because gravity stopped. Because the floor is falling away exactly as fast as you are.
That's weightlessness. Not the absence of gravity. The absence of anything pushing back.
Now the trick. Throw a ball. It curves down and hits the ground.
Throw it harder. It lands farther. Harder still — farther still.
Throw it fast enough—about eight kilometers per second—and the ground curves away beneath it just as fast as it falls. It keeps falling, and never lands.
That's an orbit. The station isn't escaping gravity. It's falling around the Earth, forever, missing the ground.
Falling, with nothing to push back. How fast must you go to keep missing the ground? Set falling equal to curving — and solve.

equivalence.concept_ar: كتلة القصور وكتلة الجاذبية ومبدأ التكافؤ
equivalence.concept_en: Inertial vs Gravitational Mass & the Equivalence Principle
equivalence.handoff_ar: Two views of the same equal masses — one calls it luck, one calls it the door to a new theory. Which experiments would tell them apart? That's the thinking we leave with you.
beats: The word mass hides two completely different ideas.
One is stubbornness. Push a thing; how hard is it to get moving? That's inertial mass.
The other is heaviness. How strongly does gravity pull on it? That's gravitational mass.
There's no reason these should be the same number. Resistance to a push, and the strength of a pull — different questions.
And yet. Galileo's old observation: drop a heavy thing and a light thing together —
— they land at the same instant. Always. Every time anyone has ever checked.
That only works if the stubbornness and the heaviness cancel perfectly. The two masses are equal.
A coincidence, said Newton. A fact you write down and use.
Not a coincidence, said Einstein. A clue.
His thought: stand in a sealed box. The floor presses your feet. Are you on Earth — or in a rocket accelerating through empty space?
From inside, you cannot tell. Gravity and acceleration feel exactly the same. That's the equivalence principle.
Two views of the same equal masses — one calls it luck, one calls it the door to a new theory. Which experiments would tell them apart? That's the thinking we leave with you.

angular.handoff_ar: Angle, rate of angle, change of that rate. Computing angular velocity and acceleration, then converting them to linear speed through the radius — that's yours.
beats: Two children on a merry-go-round. One near the center. One out at the edge.
Who's moving faster?
The one at the edge sweeps a huge circle. The one near the center barely moves. So — different speeds.
But in another sense, they move together. Same start, same finish, same instant. They never separate.
Both stories are true. We just need the right way to measure turning — not distance along the ground, but angle swept.
How much angle you've turned through: angular displacement.
How fast that angle grows: angular velocity. Measured the same for every point on the disk.
Speed it up — the angle grows faster and faster. That change is angular acceleration.
Notice the shape of this. Position becomes angle. Velocity becomes rate of angle. Acceleration becomes rate of that rate. The same trio as straight-line motion, just turned in a circle.
And the bridge between the two worlds is the radius. Same angular speed, bigger radius, faster through space.
So the child at the edge really is faster — and yet they turn at the very same rate. No contradiction.
Angle, rate of angle, change of that rate. Computing angular velocity and acceleration, then converting them to linear speed through the radius — that's yours.

torque.handoff_ar: Computing the torque from a given force and distance — and finding equilibrium when multiple torques act — that's yours.
beats: A stuck bolt. You push the wrench with everything you have. Nothing.
Then you slide a pipe over the handle to make it longer. Push gently. It turns.
Less force. More effect. Something other than force is doing the work here.
Push right at the bolt's center — and nothing turns, no matter how hard.
Push at the same spot but straight along the handle — also nothing. The direction matters too.
Turning depends on three things together: how hard, how far from the pivot, and which way.
Bundle them into one quantity. The turning effect of a force. Torque.
The distance that actually counts isn't the whole handle—it's the perpendicular distance from the pivot to where the force pushes.
That perpendicular reach has a name. The lever arm. Lengthen it, and the same force turns harder.
A door proves it daily. Push at the handle, it swings. Push next to the hinge, it fights you.
When several torques act, the rotation goes to whichever side wins the sum.
Computing the torque from a given force and distance — and finding equilibrium when multiple torques act — that's yours.

centerofmass.handoff_ar: Locating the center of mass of a given shape, and finding the maximum tilt before its vertical line leaves the base — that's yours.
beats: Throw a wrench, spinning, across the room.
Every part of it goes a different way. But one single point traces a clean, smooth arc.
That point moves as if all the mass were squeezed into it. The center of mass.
It isn't always in the middle of the object. It isn't even always inside the object.
Now stand a block on the table. Tilt it a little. It rocks back down.
Tilt it more. There's a moment where it hangs, undecided.
Past that moment, it goes over. What flipped wasn't the push. It was whether the center of mass stayed above the base.
That's the whole rule of stability. Center of mass over the base of support — it returns. Beyond it — it falls.
This is why a low, wide thing is hard to tip. A race car. A pyramid.
And why a person leans into a heavy bag — moving their center of mass back over their feet.
Lower the center, widen the base, keep it over support — that's balance, with no magic in it.
Locating the center of mass of a given shape, and finding the maximum tilt before its vertical line leaves the base — that's yours.

fictitious.handoff_ar: These forces vanish the moment you step to the still ground. When do you need them, and how big are they? That's the thinking to carry off.
beats: A car takes a sharp turn. You feel thrown against the door.
It feels like a force pushed you outward. Name it, and it seems real.
But step outside the car. Watch from above, from the still ground.
From out here, nothing pushed you outward. Your body simply wanted to go straight — and the car turned out from under you.
The outward push was never a force. It was the feeling of your own straight-line motion, seen from a turning frame.
That invented push has a name we use anyway, because it's useful inside the turning frame. The centrifugal force.
Now a harder one. Stand at the center of a spinning platform and roll a ball straight to a friend at the edge.
From above, it goes perfectly straight. From on the platform, it curves away — and misses.
Nothing bent the ball's path through space—it went perfectly straight. But the floor rotated beneath that straight path, so from the floor it looks curved.
That apparent deflection has a name. The Coriolis effect — the signature of doing physics on a turning floor.
We live on one. The Earth is the spinning platform. It steers winds and ocean currents into great curves.
These forces vanish the moment you step to the still ground. When do you need them, and how big are they? That's the thinking to carry off.

impulse.handoff_ar: Computing the impulse from a force acting over a time interval, and the change in momentum it produces — that's yours.
beats: Two cars hit a wall at the same speed. One crumples slowly. One stops instantly.
Same speed in. Same dead stop. One driver walks away. One does not.
The motion that had to be killed was identical. So what made the difference?
Call that quantity of motion momentum — mass moving, how much there is to stop.
To change momentum you need a force. But force alone isn't the whole story.
A force acting for a long time, and a much bigger force acting for an instant, can do the very same job.
It's force multiplied by the time it acts. That product is the impulse.
And the impulse equals exactly the change in momentum. That's the whole bridge.
Now the crash makes sense. Same momentum to remove. The crumple zone stretches the stopping time —
— so the force needed drops. The rigid car stops in an instant, and the force spikes.

Airbags, knees bending on landing, a boxer rolling with a punch—all the same trick. Buy time, cut the force. Watch: the airbag stretches the stop from a tenth of a second to half a second—force drops fivefold.
Computing the impulse from a force acting over a time interval, and the change in momentum it produces — that's yours.

momentumcons.handoff_ar: Setting total momentum before equal to total momentum after, and solving for an unknown velocity in a collision or recoil — that's yours.
beats: Stand on a frozen lake, perfectly still. Throw a heavy bag forward.
You slide backward. You didn't push the ground. The bag did nothing to the world but leave your hands.
Before: nothing moving. After: two things moving, opposite ways. Where did that motion come from?
Add it up the right way — motion as momentum, with direction. Forward counts as plus. Backward as minus.
The bag's forward momentum and your backward momentum are equal and opposite. They cancel.
Total momentum before: zero. Total after: still zero. Nothing was created. It only got shared.
This holds whenever no outside force interferes. A system left to itself. Isolated.
And it's just Newton's third law, looked at over time. Every push has an equal, opposite push back.
It's how a rocket climbs. Throw exhaust down hard; the rocket is thrown up just as hard.
And how collisions work. Two carts meet. Whatever one gains, the other gives up. The total never changes.
Inside the boundary, momentum is currency. It moves around. It is never spent.
Setting total momentum before equal to total momentum after, and solving for an unknown velocity in a collision or recoil — that's yours.

workenergy.handoff_ar: Computing the net work on an object and equating it to the change in its kinetic energy to find a final speed — that's yours.
beats: Push hard against a wall for an hour. You're exhausted. The wall hasn't moved.
You're exhausted—your muscles burned energy. But in physics, you did no work on the wall. None.
Work isn't effort. Work isn't being tired. Work is a force that actually moves something along its direction.
Now push a cart and let it roll. Same force — but this time it moves.
Force, times the distance it pushes through. That's work. And it isn't lost — it goes somewhere.
Where it goes: into motion. The cart that was still is now fast.
That motion is itself a kind of energy — energy of movement. The work became speed.
Do negative work — push backward, like a brake — and you take that energy out. The cart slows.
So work is the accounting of energy moving in and out of motion. Add it, motion grows. Remove it, motion shrinks.
That's the work–energy theorem. The net work done on a thing equals the change in its energy of motion. Exactly.
It's why the wall taught you nothing. No motion, no work, no change. Effort isn't in the equation.
Computing the net work on an object and equating it to the change in its kinetic energy to find a final speed — that's yours.

power.concept_en: Power — the rate of doing work
power.handoff_ar: Computing power as work divided by time — and as force times speed — for a real climb or engine — that's yours.
beats: Carry a heavy box up four flights of stairs. Take ten minutes, resting often.
Now carry the same box, up the same stairs, in twenty seconds.
Here's the thing. The work done was exactly the same. Same weight. Same height.
Same work — but the sprint nearly killed you and the stroll didn't. Work alone doesn't capture that.
What's different isn't how much. It's how fast. The same job, packed into less time.
Work divided by the time it took. That's power.
Same work, less time — more power. Same work, more time — less power. It's a rate.
It's why a small engine and a big engine can move the same car the same mile — but not in the same time.
A weak motor can lift a piano. Eventually. A strong one lifts it now. Same work, different power.
The unit honors the man who measured engines against horses. The watt.
Energy is what you transfer. Power is how fast you transfer it.
Computing power as work divided by time — and as force times speed — for a real climb or engine — that's yours.

machines.handoff_ar: Computing the mechanical advantage of a lever or ramp from its dimensions, and the input force a given load needs — that's yours.
beats: A boulder you cannot possibly lift. Slide a bar under it, rest it on a rock, and push the far end down.
The boulder rises. With a force far smaller than its weight. It feels like cheating.
It isn't. Watch your hand, not the rock. Your end traveled a long way down.
The boulder barely moved. You pushed gently — but through a huge distance.
That's the bargain every machine makes. It trades force for distance. It never gives you both.
The factor by which it multiplies your force has a name. The mechanical advantage.
A ramp is the same deal. Sliding a barrel up a long slope is easy — but you walk much farther.
So is a screw, an axle, a pulley. All of them: less force, more distance, same job underneath.
Stack them together and you get a compound machine — a bicycle, a pair of scissors, an engine.
In a perfect machine, what you put in equals what you get out. Force times distance, conserved.
The machine never makes energy. It only reshapes the deal — so a human can do a job a human couldn't.
Computing the mechanical advantage of a lever or ramp from its dimensions, and the input force a given load needs — that's yours.

efficiency.handoff_ar: Computing efficiency as useful output over total input, and the work lost to friction in a real machine — that's yours.
beats: A perfect machine: every bit of work you put in comes out the other side.
Build it in the real world and something is always missing.
You crank a real winch. Some of your effort lifts the load. Some just warms the gears.
It didn't vanish. Friction turned part of your work into heat and sound. Useless for the job.
So a machine has two stories. The work you hoped for. And the work you actually got.
The ratio of what you got to what you put in — that's efficiency.
A perfect machine would be one hundred percent. Nothing real ever is.
A good gearbox might keep ninety. A clumsy chain of parts, far less.
And in a compound machine the losses stack. Each stage skims a little off the top.
This is why ideal mechanical advantage and the real one disagree. The gap is exactly what friction ate.
Energy is still conserved — none was destroyed. It just left the job and became heat.
Computing efficiency as useful output over total input, and the work lost to friction in a real machine — that's yours.

gravpe.handoff_ar: Computing the gravitational potential energy from mass, gravity, and height above your chosen reference — that's yours.
beats: Lift a book onto a high shelf. It just sits there. Still. Doing nothing.
But you spent work lifting it. If the work goes somewhere — where did it go? The book isn't moving.
Nudge it off the edge and you find out. It falls — and arrives moving fast.
The motion it didn't have up top, it has now. The work you did was stored, waiting, the whole time.
Energy held by position. Owed motion, not yet collected. Potential energy.
Higher means more stored. Lift it twice as high, it arrives carrying twice as much.
Now a subtle question. The book on the table — does it have potential energy?
Compared to the floor, yes — it could still fall. Compared to the table top, no — it's already there.
So potential energy isn't an absolute. It's always measured from a line you choose.
That chosen line has a name. The reference level. You pick it. The physics doesn't care which.
Only the change in height matters—how far it actually falls. Set your reference at the floor, the table, or the roof: the fall distance stays the same, so the energy released is identical.
Computing the gravitational potential energy from mass, gravity, and height above your chosen reference — that's yours.

elasticpe.handoff_ar: Finding how the stored elastic energy depends on the spring's stiffness and how far it's deformed — that's yours.
beats: Pull a bow. It doesn't move. It just bends, and waits.
You did work to draw it. The bow isn't moving — so where is that work now?
Let go. The arrow leaves at terrific speed. The work was never lost. It was loaded into the bend.
Energy stored not by height — but by shape. By stretch, by squeeze, by bend.
Elastic potential energy. The energy a material holds while it's deformed and trying to spring back.
Stretch a little, store a little. Stretch more, and it fights back harder the farther you go.
So the stored energy doesn't grow steadily — it grows faster the more you deform it.
That's why a slightly longer draw on the bow sends the arrow much farther than you'd guess.
It's everywhere. A trampoline. A pole vault. The spring in a clock. A diving board.
Push too far and it won't return — the material gives, and the stored energy doesn't come back.
Within its limit, though, it's a perfect bank. Put energy in by bending; take it back when it springs.
Finding how the stored elastic energy depends on the spring's stiffness and how far it's deformed — that's yours.

rotke.handoff_ar: Computing rotational kinetic energy from the moment of inertia and the angular speed — that's yours.
beats: A spinning flywheel, going nowhere. Its center never moves.
By the usual rule, motion energy comes from a thing's speed through space. This thing's center has none.
So does a spinning wheel carry energy of motion — or not?
Try to stop it with your hand. It bites back. It clearly carries something.
Look closer. The center is still — but every other bit of the disk is racing in a circle.
Each tiny piece has its own motion energy. Add them all up, and the wheel as a whole holds plenty.
That total is rotational kinetic energy. Energy of spin, not of going anywhere.
And where the mass sits matters. The same mass, far from the axis, moves faster — and stores far more.
That reluctance to spin up or slow down has a name of its own — the moment of inertia.
It's why a flywheel can store energy, and a potter's wheel keeps turning long after the last push.
A rolling ball carries both kinds at once — moving forward and spinning. Two energies in one body.
Computing rotational kinetic energy from the moment of inertia and the angular speed — that's yours.

collisions.handoff_ar: Momentum always balances; energy of motion is the variable. Solving each type with the right pair of rules — that's yours.
beats: Two balls collide. We already know one rule never breaks: total momentum stays the same.
But momentum doesn't tell the whole story of the crash. Listen to the difference.
Two steel balls click and bounce apart, lively as before.
Two balls of clay hit and just — stop. Stuck together. Dead.
Momentum was conserved in both. So the difference isn't there. It's in the energy of motion.
The steel balls kept almost all their motion energy. We call that an elastic collision.
The clay lost most of it — to heat, to sound, to bending. An inelastic collision.
And the extreme: when they stick and move off as one. Perfectly inelastic. The most energy lost.
The energy was never destroyed. It left the motion and became heat, deformation, noise.
So a collision has two questions. Momentum: always conserved. Motion energy: only sometimes.
Real life lives in between. Most collisions keep some bounce and lose some energy.
Momentum always balances; energy of motion is the variable. Solving each type with the right pair of rules — that's yours.

tempvsthermal.handoff_ar: Average jiggle versus the whole sum — and how many particles bridges them. Quantifying that link is yours.
beats: A lit match. And a warm bathtub.
The flame is far hotter. Hundreds of degrees against a mild forty.
Now: which one could you safely touch — and which one could heat a whole room?
The match is hotter, but it can't warm the room. The cooler tub can. Hotter isn't the same as more heat.
Zoom in. Heat is really the jiggling of countless particles. Always moving, always bumping.
Temperature measures how violently each particle jiggles, on average. Just the average. Per particle.
In the match flame, each particle jiggles like mad. Very high temperature.
But there are almost none of them. A tiny flame holds very little jiggling in total.
The tub is only mildly warm — but it's a vast crowd of particles, all jiggling a little.
Add up all that jiggling and the tub holds an ocean of energy. That total is thermal energy.
Temperature is the per-particle average. Thermal energy is the grand total. Intensity versus amount.
Average jiggle versus the whole sum — and how many particles bridges them. Quantifying that link is yours.

heattransfer.handoff_ar: Which path dominates where, and how fast each moves heat — that's the thinking we hand to you.
beats: Hold a metal spoon in hot soup. Soon the handle, far from the soup, is hot too.
Nothing moved up that handle. So how did the heat travel through solid metal?
The hot end's particles jiggle hard. They knock their neighbors, who knock theirs. The jiggle passes along.
Heat passed by contact, particle to particle, the material itself staying put. That's conduction.
Now a pot of water on a flame. The bottom heats first. But the whole pot warms — and the water churns.
Hot water expands, grows light, rises. Cool water sinks to take its place. The fluid carries the heat by moving.
Heat carried by the bulk motion of a fluid. That's convection.
But step into sunlight. The Sun's heat crossed a hundred million miles of empty space. No metal. No fluid. Nothing to carry it.
It came as light. Heat that travels as radiation needs no material at all.
You feel it from a campfire across the gap, and from a hot stove before you ever touch it.
Three paths for one thing. Touch. Flow. Across nothing at all.
Which path dominates where, and how fast each moves heat — that's the thinking we hand to you.

specificheat.handoff_ar: Computing the heat needed from the specific heat, the mass, and the temperature change — that's yours.
beats: Same flame. Same time. Under one pot of water — and one pot of cooking oil.
Equal heat poured into each. But the oil is scorching while the water is merely warm.
Same energy in. Wildly different temperature out. Why does water resist heating up?
Some substances soak up a lot of energy for only a small rise in temperature.
Others heat fast — a little energy sends their temperature shooting up.
That stubbornness — how much energy one gram needs to warm by one degree — is the specific heat.
Water's is famously huge. It's one of the hardest common things to heat — and to cool.
That's why the sea is mild while the desert sand next to it bakes by noon and freezes by night.
It's why water cools an engine, why a hot-water bottle stays warm for hours.
And it's why your own body, mostly water, holds a steady temperature through a changing day.
Heat needed depends on three things: the substance, how much of it, and how big a rise you want.
Computing the heat needed from the specific heat, the mass, and the temperature change — that's yours.

latentheat.concept_en: Latent Heat — energy hidden in a change of state
latentheat.lesson_title_ar: تغيرات حالة المادة وقوانين الديناميكا الحرارية
latentheat.handoff_ar: Computing the heat absorbed during melting and boiling from the mass and the latent heat — that's yours.
beats: Heat a glass of ice water and watch the thermometer.
You're pouring energy in steadily. But while ice remains, the temperature does not budge. Zero. Still zero.
Energy in, no temperature rise. The heat is going somewhere we can't see on the thermometer.
Zoom in. In ice, particles are locked in a rigid lattice. The incoming energy isn't speeding them up —
— it's breaking the bonds that hold them in place. Energy spent on freedom, not on speed.
Only once the last of the ice is freed does the thermometer start climbing again.
That hidden energy — paid to change state, not to change temperature — is the latent heat.
The same pause happens again at boiling. Energy floods in to tear liquid into vapor — temperature holds flat.
And it runs in reverse. Steam releases that hidden energy when it condenses—which is why steam delivers far more heat to skin than boiling water at the same temperature.
It's why sweat cools you: evaporating water carries off enormous hidden energy as it leaves your skin.
A full heating curve, then, has flats and slopes. Slopes warm it. Flats rebuild it.
Computing the heat absorbed during melting and boiling from the mass and the latent heat — that's yours.

thermolaws.lesson_title_ar: تغيرات حالة المادة وقوانين الديناميكا الحرارية
thermolaws.handoff_ar: Computing an engine's maximum possible efficiency from its hot and cold operating temperatures — that's yours.
beats: Two rules govern every engine, every fridge, every living thing. They sound almost too simple.
First rule. Energy is never created and never destroyed. It only changes form, or moves.
Heat into an engine becomes work out, plus heat dumped away. Add it all back: nothing missing. Ever.
So the first rule says you can't win — you can't get more energy out than you put in.
Then the second rule, the strange one. It's about direction.
Drop ink in water; it spreads. You will never, ever watch it gather itself back into a drop.
A hot coffee always cools to the room. The room never spontaneously reheats the coffee.
Nothing in the first rule forbids the reverse. Energy would still balance. Yet it never happens.
Because the universe drifts, always, toward more spread-out, more disordered, more mixed. We measure that drift with entropy.
The second rule: total entropy always increases. That's why time has a direction.
It's why no engine is perfect. Some energy must always be paid out as disordered waste heat. You can't even break even.
Computing an engine's maximum possible efficiency from its hot and cold operating temperatures — that's yours.

projects.project_ar: المنحدر الذي ظلّ يكذب عليه — تدقيق طاقة في حديقة التزلج
projects.project_en: The Ramp That Kept Lying to Him — a Skate Park Energy Audit
projects.persona: Faris — building a plywood launch ramp to clear a gap
projects.ending_hint: Diegetic callback to the concept-spark videos (book on the shelf, the cart, the pendulum) — the hint IS the last movement of the story.
beats: Faris built a ramp out of plywood. A launch on one side, a gap, a landing on the other.
One goal. Clear the gap. Land clean on the far side.
He starts low. He doesn't make it. He falls into the gap.
So he builds the launch higher. Now he overshoots — flies past the landing, and crashes.
He lowers it a little. Short again.
He decides the wood is the problem. He sands it smooth. He waxes it.
Same height as before. But now he flies further — and overshoots again. The same ramp gave him two different answers.
He raises, lowers, sands, guesses. He is almost out of plywood, and out of patience.
The ramp keeps lying to him. Same setup, different result. He can't see the rule underneath.
But he was never short of wood. He was short of a model.
Every failed run was energy he didn't count. The height he started from — that became his speed at the bottom.
And the rough wood quietly ate some of it — turned it into heat. Wax the ramp, less is eaten, he goes further.
Same height, two answers — because friction was a term he never wrote down.
So before he touches the real ramp again — he tests it somewhere he can see the energy.
Energy Skate Park. He builds his ramp's shape into the track. Turns the energy bars on.
Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before he does.
How fast at the bottom. How high on the far side. Worked out from the energy — start height, minus what friction takes.
Then he presses play. And he watches the bars, not the skater.
After — he explains the gap. If he said this and the bars said that: which lever did he misjudge? The height? The friction?
He adjusts the friction. Predicts again. Three runs. That's all he gets.
His first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells him which energy he forgot.
Now he builds the real ramp. Once. And the landing is clean.
The clean landing was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — why did the same ramp give two answers, and which energy did he keep forgetting?

And if all of this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the book that sat still on the shelf, holding energy it hadn't spent? The cart that turned a push into speed? The pendulum that never lost a thing — until something quietly took it?
This ramp was all of them, at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.

projects.project_ar: تحقيق في تصادم — مَن كان أسرعَ حقًّا؟
projects.project_en: Collision Forensics — who was really going faster?
projects.persona: Noura — two toy carts, one dent, an argument to settle
projects.ending_hint: Diegetic callback to impulse (crumple/airbag), momentum conservation (the bag throw / rocket), collisions (steel vs clay).
beats: Two toy carts collided on the table. One ended up dented. Noura's little brother says his cart was barely moving.
She wants one answer. Who was actually going faster, the moment they hit?
She looks at the dent. The bigger dent must mean the harder hit. Right?
So she guesses from the wreck. She's sure the dented one took the bigger hit, so it was the slower one.
Her brother runs it again. Same carts. This time the dented one was clearly the fast one. Her rule just broke.
She tries reading speed from how far they slid after. But a heavy cart and a light cart slide differently from the same hit.
Every clue lies on its own. The dent, the slide, the noise — none of them, alone, gives her the speed.
She wasn't short of clues. She was short of a conserved quantity — something the crash can't change.
Mass times velocity, added up for both carts. Before the hit and after — that total stays the same. Always.
And the energy of motion? That can drop — into the dent, the sound, the heat. How much it drops tells her what kind of collision this was.
Two rules. One that never changes. One that's allowed to. Together they pin down what really happened.
Before she argues another minute — she rebuilds the crash where she can measure it.
Collision Lab. She sets the two masses. Turns on the momentum arrows and the kinetic-energy number.
Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before she does.
Given what each cart does after — work backward. What speed must each have had before, so the total momentum balances?
Then she presses play. And she watches the arrows and the energy number — not the carts.
After — she explains the gap. If the energy barely dropped, they bounced. If it collapsed, they crushed together.
She adjusts a mass. Predicts again. Three runs. That's all she gets.
Her first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells her which clue had fooled her.
Now she answers her brother. Not from the dent — from the numbers. And she can show why.
The argument was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — which clue lied, and which rule never did?
And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the two cars at the wall, one that crumpled slowly and one that didn't?
And the figure on the frozen lake, who threw a bag and slid backward — giving nothing away that the ice could keep?
And the steel that bounced, and the clay that just stopped — same momentum, different energy?
This crash was all three at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.

projects.project_ar: ابْنِ مدارًا مستقرًّا — دورةٌ واحدةٌ نظيفة، دون اصطدام
projects.project_en: Build a Stable Orbit — one clean lap, no crash
projects.persona: Sami — trying to make a moon circle his planet exactly once
projects.ending_hint: Diegetic callback to gravitation (two rocks pulling), gfield (arrows around Earth), weightless (throw fast enough, never lands).
beats: Sami wants a moon to circle his planet. Just once, clean, and come back around.
One goal. One full lap. Not into the planet. Not off into the dark.
He releases it gently. It falls straight in and hits the planet.
So he throws it harder. Now it tears away in a straight line and never comes back.
He eases off. It curves, hope rises — then sags inward and crashes anyway.
Faster, slower, faster. Sometimes an egg shape, sometimes a crash. He's clicking, not choosing.
He wasn't short of tries. He was short of the balance underneath — fall against fling.
The planet always pulls the moon straight toward its center. That pull never rests.
And the moon, left alone, only wants to go straight. An orbit is those two, matched.
Too slow, the pull wins — it falls in. Too fast, the straight line wins — it escapes. The lap lives in between.
It is the same thing as throwing a ball so fast the ground keeps curving away beneath it.
Before he clicks again — he builds it where he can see the pull and the path.
Gravity and Orbits. He sets the masses and the distance. Turns the force arrow and the path trace on.
Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before he does.
What launch speed makes the pull and the straight line match — so the trace closes into a circle, not a spiral, not an escape?
Then he presses play. And he watches the trace, not the moon.
After — he explains the gap. Spiraled in? Launch was slow. Flew off? Too fast. The trace names his error.
He adjusts the speed. Predicts again. Three runs. That's all he gets.
His first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss narrows the speed.
The closed circle was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — why does staying in orbit mean falling forever without landing?
And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the two rocks that pulled each other across empty space, with nothing in between?
And the Earth that changed the space around it — arrows crowding inward, everywhere, whether anything was there to feel them or not?
And the ball thrown so fast the ground curved away beneath it — falling, and never landing?
This orbit was all three at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.

projects.project_ar: حاملُ التوازن — حاملٌ مُعلَّقٌ لا يميل
projects.project_en: The Balancing Rig — a mobile that won't tip
projects.persona: Lina — building a hanging mobile that keeps tilting
projects.ending_hint: Diegetic callback to torque (wrench / lever arm) and center of mass (tumbling wrench, tilting block over its base).
beats: Lina is hanging a mobile over her little brother's bed. A bar, a string at the middle, shapes dangling off each side.
One goal. It hangs level. Not nose-down on one side.
She hangs the shapes. The bar swings hard to the right and stays there.
So she moves a heavy shape to the left. Now it slams over the other way.
She adds a small shape near the string. It barely changes anything. She doesn't see why.
Heavy near the middle does almost nothing. Light far out swings it a lot. Same weights, opposite effects.
She wasn't short of shapes. She was short of the thing that decides tilt — not weight, but weight times its reach.
Every shape twists the bar by its weight times its distance from the string. That twist is what has to balance.
Level means the left twists and the right twists cancel. Exactly. The total turning effect is zero.
It is the same rule as a long wrench: reach matters as much as force. And it is where the whole thing balances — its center of mass — sitting right under the string.
Before she re-hangs another shape — she builds it where she can see the twist.
Balancing Act. She places the masses at marked distances. Turns the level indicator on.
Here is the real job. Not letting go of the plank. Predicting — before she does.
Where must the last mass go so the left and right twists match — and the plank stays level when she lets go?
Then she lets go. And she watches the level indicator, not the bricks.
After — she explains the gap. Tipped right? The right twist won. By how much tells her how far she was off.
She moves one mass. Predicts again. Three tries. That's all she gets.
Her first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells her which reach she misjudged.
Now she hangs the real mobile. Once. And it floats level over the bed.
The level mobile was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — why did moving a light shape outward beat moving a heavy one inward?
And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the stuck bolt, and how a longer wrench turned it with a gentler push — reach against force?
And the tumbling wrench whose one quiet point traced a clean arc — the place a thing balances, whether or not it's in the middle?
This rig was both at once — twist, and the point it all balances on. That is the concept.
Now you know which videos to go back to.

projects.project_ar: آلةُ النابض والإسقاط — اقرعِ الجرسَ عند الارتفاع الصحيح
projects.project_en: The Spring-Drop Machine — ring the bell at the right height
projects.persona: Yousef — a spring launcher that must ring a bell at an exact height
projects.ending_hint: Diegetic callback to elastic PE (bow/spring), gravitational PE (book on shelf), kinetic energy (the cart), conservation (the pendulum).
beats: Yousef built a spring launcher. Press a ball onto the spring, release, and it should rise and tap a bell.
One goal. The ball just reaches the bell — taps it — and no higher.
He presses the spring a little. The ball pops up and falls short, well below the bell.
So he presses it down hard. The ball flies way past the bell and keeps going.
He eases off a little — and it still overshoots, by more than the easing should explain.
A heavier ball with the same press barely clears his hand. Same spring, completely different height.
He wasn't short of presses. He was short of an account — where the squeeze goes, and what it has to become.
The squeeze stores energy in the spring. Let go, and it becomes the ball's speed.
And the speed buys height — trading itself away as the ball climbs, until none is left and it stops at the top.
Spring energy becomes motion becomes height. The same total, just changing form — and a heavier ball turns the same total into less height.
Before he presses again — he builds it where he can see the energy move.
Masses and Springs. He sets the spring stiffness and the mass. Turns the energy bars on.
Here is the real job. Not releasing the spring. Predicting — before he does.
How far must he compress it so the stored energy becomes exactly the height of the bell — for this ball's mass?
Then he releases. And he watches the bars hand energy along, not the ball.
After — he explains the gap. Overshot? Too much stored. Fell short? Too little. The bars name the error.
He adjusts the compression. Predicts again. Three tries. That's all he gets.
His first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells him which form he mismeasured.
Now he sets the real launcher. Once. The ball rises, taps the bell, and falls back.
The bell was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — trace one unit of energy from the squeeze to the top.
And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the drawn bow that didn't move, holding energy in its bend?
And the book on the high shelf, holding energy it hadn't spent? And the cart that turned a push into pure speed?
And the pendulum that never lost a thing — only kept trading it, back and forth?
This machine was all of them at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.

projects.project_ar: ميزانيّةُ الحرارة — أَذِبْها في اللحظة الصحيحة
projects.project_en: The Thermal Budget — melt it at the right moment
projects.persona: Maha — must turn a block of ice to water exactly when the timer ends
projects.ending_hint: Diegetic callback to temperature-vs-thermal (match & tub), specific heat (water vs oil), heat transfer (spoon/pot/Sun), latent heat (the thermometer stuck at zero).
beats: Maha has a block of ice, a small heater, and a timer. The block must be fully water exactly when the timer ends.
One goal. Not melted early. Not still icy at the end. Done, right on the buzzer.
She turns the heater high. The thermometer races up, then stalls dead at zero while the ice just sits there.
She thinks the heater died. She turns it higher. The needle still won't move off zero.
Then, all at once, the ice is gone and the temperature shoots up — and overshoots, boiling before the buzzer.
She swaps to a metal cup to help. It heats the water faster than she expects and ruins her timing again.
She wasn't short of heat. She was short of a budget — energy has more than one bill to pay here.
Some energy raises temperature — and how much it takes depends on the substance and how much there is.
And a huge chunk pays only to change state — melting the ice — with the thermometer flat the entire time.
And the rate the bill arrives depends on what carries the heat — fast through metal, slow otherwise.
Hidden melt cost, temperature cost, delivery rate. Miss one line and the timing is always wrong.
Before she touches the heater again — she builds it where she can see every line of the budget.
Energy Forms and Changes. She sets the heat rate and the amount. Turns the energy chunks and the thermometer on.
Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before she does.
What heat rate makes the temperature cost plus the hidden melt cost finish exactly when the timer hits zero?
Then she presses play. And she watches the energy chunks and the flat stretch, not the ice.
After — she explains the gap. Finished early? She underpaid the hidden melt. Late? She overpaid the rest.
She adjusts the heat rate. Predicts again. Three tries. That's all she gets.
Her first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss exposes a line she forgot.
The melt-on-time was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — where did the energy go while the thermometer stood still?
And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the tiny match and the warm tub — hotter is not the same as more heat?
And the water that drank energy and barely warmed, while the oil shot up — the same heat, different substance?
And the spoon, the churning pot, the Sun across empty space — three ways heat ever arrives? And the thermometer that stuck at zero while the ice quietly drank it all?
This melt was all four at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.
